layer.scale: Highlights scale tones on the fretboard. When you change the root note, the same scale shape shifts accordingly.

chord.form: Displays barre chord and open chord forms with 6th-string and 5th-string roots on the fretboard.
chord.triad: Displays triads on 3-string sets with inversions. Great for learning chord tones and arpeggio practice.
chord.onChord: Displays slash chord (on-chord) voicings. Changing the bass note creates different textures and smooth voice leading.

layer.caged: The CAGED system maps 5 open chord shapes (C, A, G, E, D) across the entire fretboard. It helps you systematically understand chord tone positions everywhere.
layer.custom: Freely select notes or degrees to display on the fretboard. Useful for examining specific intervals or note combinations.
layer.progression: Displays a chord progression step by step. Keep other layers fixed and swap only the chord — perfect for comparing scale/CAGED patterns across a progression.

progression.145: The most fundamental progression in blues and rock. Tonic→Subdominant→Dominant — appears in virtually every genre.
progression.251: The core jazz progression. Subdominant (ii)→Dominant (V)→Tonic (I) gives a clear sense of resolution. The starting point for jazz phrasing and modulation practice.
progression.1625: A classic jazz turnaround. I→vi→ii→V cycles endlessly and appears constantly in jazz standards.
progression.1645: The 50s pop / doo-wop progression. I→VI→IV→V has a bright, nostalgic feel found in countless oldies.
progression.pop: The most widely used progression in modern pop. I→V→vi→IV loops with a blend of brightness and wistfulness.
progression.3625: A jazz turnaround with a pickup. iii→VI→ii→V flows smoothly and frequently appears in the A sections of standards.
progression.1341m: A progression using the minor subdominant (iv). The III between I and IV, plus the final iv, creates a distinctive shadow. Common in rock and pop.
progression.minor251: The minor ii-V-i. The V chord is a dominant 7th from the harmonic minor scale, strengthening the sense of resolution. Ubiquitous in jazz and bossa nova.
progression.andalusian: The Andalusian cadence — a flamenco and rock staple. The descending i→VII line evokes melancholy with a Phrygian feel.
progression.blues: The standard 12-bar blues form. I→IV→I→V→IV→I — the foundation of blues and rock.
progression.diatonicMajorTriad: All seven diatonic triads in a major key. Rather than playing through as a progression, use it to step through I, IIm, IIIm, IV, V, VIm, VII° and map each chord's shape to its function in the key.
progression.diatonicMajorSeventh: All seven diatonic 7th chords in a major key. Rather than playing through as a progression, step through Imaj7, IIm7, V7 and the rest to see what tensions each degree carries in the key.
progression.diatonicMinorTriad: All seven diatonic triads in a natural minor key. Rather than playing through as a progression, use it to step through Im, II°, ♭III, IVm, Vm, ♭VI, ♭VII and map each chord's shape to its function in the key.
progression.diatonicMinorSeventh: All seven diatonic 7th chords in a natural minor key. Rather than playing through as a progression, step through Im7, IIm7♭5, ♭IIImaj7, IVm7, Vm7, ♭VImaj7, ♭VII7 to see what tensions each degree carries in the key.

scale.major:
A bright, stable scale. The most fundamental scale in pop and classical music.
Formula: R, M2, M3, P4, P5, M6, M7
scale.naturalMinor:
A dark, melancholic scale. The foundation of minor keys.
Formula: R, M2, m3, P4, P5, m6, m7
scale.majorPenta:
A 5-note scale from the major scale (no 4th or 7th). Used for bright phrases in country and pop.
Formula: R, M2, M3, P5, M6
scale.minorPenta:
A 5-note scale from the minor scale (no 2nd or 6th). The most used scale in rock and blues.
Formula: R, m3, P4, P5, m7
scale.blues:
Minor pentatonic plus the ♭5 (blue note). Creates the gritty, soulful blues sound.
Formula: R, m3, P4, ♭5, P5, m7
scale.harmonicMinor:
Natural minor with a raised 7th. Enables V7 chords for stronger resolution in minor keys.
Formula: R, M2, m3, P4, P5, m6, M7
scale.melodicMinor:
Natural minor with raised 6th and 7th (ascending). Used symmetrically in jazz.
Formula: R, M2, m3, P4, P5, M6, M7
scale.ionian:
Same as the major scale. The 1st mode, bright and stable.
Formula: R, M2, M3, P4, P5, M6, M7
scale.dorian:
The brightest minor mode. Frequently used in funk and jazz minor tunes.
Formula: R, M2, m3, P4, P5, M6, m7
scale.phrygian:
A Spanish-flavored mode. The ♭2 gives it a distinctive flamenco or metal sound.
Formula: R, m2, m3, P4, P5, m6, m7
scale.lydian:
A floating, dreamy mode with #4. Common in film scores and fusion.
Formula: R, M2, M3, #4, P5, M6, M7
scale.mixolydian:
Major scale with a ♭7. A staple of blues rock and funk.
Formula: R, M2, M3, P4, P5, M6, m7
scale.aeolian:
Same as natural minor. The 6th mode.
Formula: R, M2, m3, P4, P5, m6, m7
scale.locrian:
The darkest, most unstable mode. The ♭5 makes it suited for diminished chords.
Formula: R, m2, m3, P4, ♭5, m6, m7
scale.phrygianDominant:
Phrygian with a major 3rd. An exotic, Arabic-sounding scale.
Formula: R, m2, M3, P4, P5, m6, m7
scale.lydianDominant:
Lydian with a ♭7. Creates a floating sound over dominant 7th chords.
Formula: R, M2, M3, #4, P5, M6, m7
scale.altered:
Used over dominant 7th chords. All tensions are altered, creating strong tension.
Formula: R, m2, #2, M3, ♭5, m6, m7
scale.wholeTone:
A symmetrical 6-note scale with all whole steps. Creates an ambiguous, dream-like quality.
Formula: R, M2, M3, #4, #5, m7
scale.diminished:
An 8-note scale alternating whole and half steps. Used over diminished and dominant chords.
Formula: R, M2, m3, P4, ♭5, m6, M6, M7

chord.Major:
A bright, stable basic triad.
Formula: R, M3, P5
chord.Minor:
A dark, melancholic basic triad.
Formula: R, m3, P5
chord.7th:
A major triad with ♭7 — the dominant 7th. Essential in blues and jazz.
Formula: R, M3, P5, m7
chord.maj7:
A major triad with M7. A soft, sophisticated sound.
Formula: R, M3, P5, M7
chord.m7:
A minor triad with ♭7. A dark, relaxed jazz staple.
Formula: R, m3, P5, m7
chord.m7b5:
Also called half-diminished. Common as the ii chord in minor keys.
Formula: R, m3, ♭5, m7
chord.dim7:
A symmetrical chord built entirely of minor 3rds. A tense passing chord.
Formula: R, m3, ♭5, dim7
chord.mmaj7:
A minor triad with M7. A mysterious, haunting sound used in jazz ballads.
Formula: R, m3, P5, M7
chord.sus2:
Replaces the 3rd with 2nd for an open, floating quality.
Formula: R, M2, P5
chord.sus4:
Replaces the 3rd with 4th for a suspended, tense quality that wants to resolve.
Formula: R, P4, P5
chord.7sus4:
A sus4 chord with ♭7. Common in funk and R&B.
Formula: R, P4, P5, m7
chord.6:
A major triad with added 6th. A jazzy, nostalgic sound.
Formula: R, M3, P5, M6
chord.m6:
A minor triad with added 6th.
Formula: R, m3, P5, M6
chord.dim:
Two stacked minor 3rds. An unstable triad.
Formula: R, m3, ♭5
chord.aug:
Two stacked major 3rds. A tense, unresolved triad.
Formula: R, M3, #5
chord.9:
Dominant 7th with added 9th. A funky, rich sound.
Formula: R, M3, P5, m7, M9
chord.b9:
Dominant 7th with ♭9. A tense, dramatic chord.
Formula: R, M3, P5, m7, m9
chord.sharp9:
Dominant 7th with #9. Also known as the Hendrix chord.
Formula: R, M3, P5, m7, #9
chord.add9:
Major triad with just the 9th added (no 7th).
Formula: R, M3, P5, M9
chord.madd9:
Minor triad with just the 9th added (no 7th).
Formula: R, m3, P5, M2
chord.11:
Dominant 7th with 9th and 11th. A thick, rich voicing.
Formula: R, M3, P5, m7, M9, P11
chord.sharp11:
Dominant 7th with #11. A Lydian-flavored sound.
Formula: R, M3, P5, m7, #11
chord.add11:
Major triad with just the 11th added.
Formula: R, M3, P5, P11
chord.addSharp11:
Major triad with just #11 added.
Formula: R, M3, P5, #11
chord.13:
Dominant 7th with 9th and 13th. A lush, colorful chord.
Formula: R, M3, P5, m7, M9, M13
chord.b13:
Dominant 7th with ♭13. A darker tension chord.
Formula: R, M3, P5, m7, ♭13
chord.Diminished:
A triad built from two stacked minor 3rds. Unstable.
Formula: R, m3, ♭5
chord.Augmented:
A triad built from two stacked major 3rds. Tense.
Formula: R, M3, #5
chord.5:
A two-note chord using only the root and 5th. Also known as a power chord, it's widely used in rock and punk for its simple, powerful sound.
Formula: R, P5

finder.longPressInstruction: Long press the fretboard to select root note
finder.tapInstruction: Tap the fretboard to add notes
finder.emptyHint: Select notes to see matching results
finder.addToLayerFull: You can have up to 3 layers. Remove an existing layer to add a new one.
finder.setNotesAlreadySet: These notes are already set on the fretboard.
finder.homeIdentifyDesc: Find chords and scales from selected notes
finder.homeChordListDesc: Find a chord by its shape on the fretboard
finder.homeDiatonicDesc: Browse diatonic chords for any key

diatonicFunction.tonicDesc: A stable function that provides resolution and a sense of rest. Acts as the home base of the key.
diatonicFunction.subdominantDesc: A function between tonic and dominant. Adds movement and color to a progression.
diatonicFunction.dominantDesc: A tense function that strongly resolves to the tonic. Contains the leading tone a half-step below the root.

finder.homeSubDesc: Find substitute chords for any chord

substitution.none: No substitutions for this chord in this key
substitution.tonicDesc: Chords sharing two common tones are interchangeable as tonic substitutes. I, VIm (relative minor), and IIIm (mediant) can substitute for each other. E.g., C ↔ Am ↔ Em.
substitution.subdominantDesc: IIm (IIm7) and IV (IVmaj7) are interchangeable subdominant function chords that share two common tones. Substitution works in both directions. E.g., Dm ↔ F.
substitution.dominantDesc: A dominant 7th chord and the 7th chord a tritone (6 semitones) away are interchangeable. Both share the same guide tones — the 3rd and 7th swap roles — allowing smooth half-step voice leading in the bass. E.g., G7 ↔ D♭7.

finder.homePivotChordDesc: Find common chords (pivot chords) between two keys
finder.homeModesDesc: Explore mode families and modal interchange chords

desc.relative: Shares the same key signature but has a different tonic. The relative minor starts a minor 3rd below the major root — same notes, different feel.
desc.parallel: Shares the same tonic but has a different key signature. Switching between parallel keys creates a dramatic shift between brightness and darkness.
desc.dominant: The key built on the 5th degree. Moving to the dominant creates forward momentum and tension that strongly wants to resolve back home.
desc.subdominant: The key built on the 4th degree. Moving to the subdominant creates a warm, expansive contrast before returning to the home key.

modes.allShared: No borrowed chords from this mode

finder.homeProgressionAnalysisDesc: Analyze key and chord functions from a progression
finder.homeScaleCompatDesc: Find scales that fit a chord

scaleCompat.empty: Add chords to find compatible scales
scaleCompat.noResult: No compatible scales for this combination

secondaryDominant.secDomDesc: A secondary dominant is a dominant 7th chord that temporarily resolves to a diatonic chord other than the tonic. By treating the target chord as a momentary tonic and playing its V7, it creates strong forward motion.

keyFromChords.empty: Add chords to find the key

finder.homeSecondaryDominantDesc: Find secondary dominant chords for any key
finder.homeKeyFinderDesc: Identify the key from a set of chords
finder.homeTensionAvoidDesc: Find tensions and avoid notes for a chord in a key
finder.homeOnChordDesc: Filter slash chord voicings by root note and type
finder.homeCircleDesc: Visualize key relationships, diatonic chords, and secondary dominants
finder.homeChordSuggestDesc: Browse next-chord candidates by category for any chord
finder.homeDominantMotionDesc: Explore dominant resolution patterns from V→I to backdoor cadences
finder.homeModulationTargetDesc: Browse closely related keys and their shared chords
finder.homeModulationMeansDesc: Find modulation methods between two keys

chordSuggest.chainHint: → Tap a chord to add it

desc.basicVI: The most fundamental dominant motion: V7 resolves to I. The tritone between the 3rd (leading tone) and 7th of V7 creates a strong pull — the leading tone rises a half step to the root, and the 7th falls a half step to the 3rd of I.
desc.twoFiveOne: The ii7 → V7 → I progression. ii7 acts as subdominant preparation, V7 creates tension, and I resolves it. The cornerstone of jazz harmony, it produces a natural flow in any key.
desc.secondaryDom: A V7 chord that resolves to a diatonic chord other than the tonic. By treating the target chord as a temporary tonic, it creates a sense of momentary modulation and strong forward motion.
desc.tritoneSub: A technique replacing V7 with a ♭II7 chord whose root is a tritone (6 semitones) away. Though their 3rds and 7ths are swapped, the bass descends by half step, creating smooth voice leading.
desc.backdoor: A resolution from ♭VII7 (subdominant minor region) to the tonic. Unlike the standard V7, this backdoor cadence brings a flatter, warmer character to the resolution.
desc.dominantChain: A chained progression V/V → V → I, also called the double dominant. Tension builds step by step toward the final resolution to I.
desc.dimResolution: The leading-tone diminished 7th chord (vii°7) resolving to I. Every voice moves by half step into a chord tone of the tonic, producing exceptionally smooth voice leading.

stats.title: Weak Points

quiz.questionDiatonicAll: What are the diatonic chords in {{root}} {{keyType}} / {{chordSize}}?
